points: Two figures are similar if they have the same shape but not necessarily the same size. For similar polygons: (i) corresponding angles are equal, and (ii) corresponding sides are in the same ratio (scale factor).
Two triangles are similar if they satisfy one of the criteria: AAA (all angles equal), AA (two angles equal), SSS (all sides proportional), or SAS (one angle equal and including sides proportional).
The Thales' Theorem (Basic Proportionality Theorem) states: if a line parallel to one side of a triangle intersects the other two sides, it divides them in the same ratio. The converse is also true.

summary.category: Properties of Similar Triangles and Scale Factor
points: If two triangles are similar with scale factor k, then: (i) the ratio of corresponding sides is k, (ii) the ratio of perimeters is k, and (iii) the ratio of areas is k².
Linear dimensions (sides, altitudes, medians, radii) of similar figures scale by the scale factor k, while areas scale by k².
Similar triangles allow indirect measurements: finding heights of tall objects, distances to far objects, and solving practical geometry problems without direct measurement.
Pythagoras Theorem: In a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of squares of the other two sides: c² = a² + b².
Converse of Pythagoras Theorem: If c² = a² + b² in a triangle with sides a, b, c, then the triangle is right-angled with the right angle opposite side c.
Pythagoras theorem can be proved using the similarity of triangles formed when an altitude is drawn from the right angle to the hypotenuse.
All congruent figures are similar with scale factor 1. Not all similar figures are congruent; congruence requires equal size in addition to equal shape.
For problem-solving: identify similar triangles using the criteria, establish correspondence, use proportionality of sides to find unknown lengths, and apply area relationships.
Practical applications include measuring heights using shadows and similar triangles, determining distances indirectly, and verifying right angles using Pythagoras theorem or its converse.
